---
title: "Practical Exercise: Exploring Relationships in the Homework Dataset"
subtitle: "SOST10142/20142 Applied Statistics for Social Scientists"
author: "Tanja"
format: 
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    fontsize: 11pt
    tbl-colwidths: [50, 110]
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

In this practical you will analyse data from the study:

Smith, C. (2025). *The Impact of Homework Deadline Times on College Student Performance and Stress: A Quasi-Experiment in Business Statistics*. Journal of Statistics and Data Science Education, 33(3), 334–343. <https://doi.org/10.1080/26939169.2024.2441692>

The dataset contains information about students enrolled in a statistics course, including:

-   time spent on homework
-   homework grades
-   course grades
-   GPA
-   homework deadline policies

The goal of this exercise is to explore **relationships between variables** and begin thinking about **which factors may influence student performance**.

In the previous lecture we examined the relationship between:

Homework time $\rightarrow$ Homework grade

In this practical you will investigate **other relationships in the dataset**.

Next week we will extend these ideas by fitting a **multiple regression model**.

## Dataset Variables

The dataset contains several variables describing student behaviour, background characteristics, and academic performance.

| Variable | Description |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **ID** | Unique identifier for each student |
| **HW_minutes** | Total number of minutes the student spent on homework |
| **Midnight_deadline** | Indicator variable showing whether homework deadlines were set at midnight (1 = midnight deadline, 0 = earlier deadline) |
| **Fall_semester** | Indicator variable showing whether the course took place in the fall semester (1 = fall semester, 0 = other semester) |
| **Female** | Gender indicator (1 = female, 0 = male) |
| **Section** | Course section number |
| **Year_in_school** | Student’s academic year (1 = first year, 2 = second year, 3 = third year, 4 = fourth year) |
| **GPA** | Student’s grade point average |
| **ACT** | ACT entrance exam score (some values may be missing) |
| **Grade_course** | Student’s final course grade |
| **Grade_HW** | Student’s homework grade |

### Academic Major Indicators

| Variable             | Description                                     |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| **Major_BA**         | Business Administration major (1 = yes, 0 = no) |
| **Major_Finance**    | Finance major                                   |
| **Major_Accounting** | Accounting major                                |
| **Major_Marketing**  | Marketing major                                 |
| **Major_Management** | Management major                                |
| **Major_Sport**      | Sport management major                          |

### Student Survey Variables

The following survey questions are measured on a **5-point Likert scale**, where higher values indicate stronger agreement.

| Value | Interpretation    |
|-------|-------------------|
| 1     | Strongly disagree |
| 2     | Disagree          |
| 3     | Neutral           |
| 4     | Agree             |
| 5     | Strongly agree    |

| Variable | Description |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Q1_HW_effective** | Student perception of how effective homework is for learning (Likert scale 1–5) |
| **Q2_deadline_effect** | Perceived effect of deadline timing on homework completion (Likert scale 1–5) |
| **Q3_deadline_stress** | Degree to which homework deadlines create stress (Likert scale 1–5) |
| **Q4_average_time** | Student-reported average time spent on homework (minutes; stored as text in the dataset) |
| **Q5_preferred_time** | Preferred time of day for completing homework |
| **Q6_extensions** | Number of homework extensions requested |
| **Q7_late_turnins** | Number of late homework submissions |

Take a moment to read through the variable descriptions.

**Which variables do you think might help explain differences in student grades?**

Load the dataset and fit the regression model used in the lecture.

```{r}
library(dplyr)
hw <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/TanjaKec/mydata/master/HW_R.csv")
glimpse(hw)
summary(hw)

model_hw<- lm(Grade_HW~ HW_minutes,data =hw)
summary(model_hw)

```

[**Question 1**]{style="color:red;"}

What does the positive slope in the regression model suggest about the relationship between homework time and homework grades?

\vspace{3cm}

[**Question 2** ]{style="color:red;"}

The value of ($R^2$) in the model is approximately 0.065.

What does this tell us about how much variation in homework grades is explained by homework time?

\vspace{3cm}

## Testing for the Existence of a Relationship

The value of $R^2$ tells us how much variation in the response variable is explained by the model.

However, we still need to determine whether the observed relationship is statistically meaningful.

To answer this question, we use a hypothesis test based on the F-statistic.

For the regression model relating **Grade_HW** to **HW_minutes**, we test:

$$
H_0 : \beta_1 = 0
$$

$$
H_1 : \beta_1 \neq 0
$$

::: callout-note
**Writing hypotheses in a report**

In this practical we write hypotheses in a simplified form.\
In a statistical report you would usually describe them more clearly in words and explain their meaning.

For the example relating **homework grade** to **homework time**, the hypotheses are:

$$
H_0 : \beta_1 = 0
$$

There is **no linear relationship** between homework time and homework grade.

$$
H_1 : \beta_1 \neq 0
$$

There **is a linear relationship** between homework time and homework grade.

In a report you would typically explain what this means in context.\
For example, rejecting $(H_0)$ would suggest that the amount of time students spend on homework is related to their homework performance.
:::

The decision rule for the F-test is shown below.

```{r}
#| label: f_test_decision_rule
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "500px"

knitr::include_graphics("images/F-test.png")
```

To evaluate this hypothesis test, we examine the output of the fitted regression model. The regression summary contains the **F-statistic**, which is used to test whether the explanatory variable helps explain variation in the response variable.

To examine the statistical results of the fitted regression model, we now look at the regression summary to examine the statistical summary of the fitted regression model.

The regression summary provides information about the estimated coefficients, the value of $R^2$, and the F-statistic used for hypothesis testing.

Run the following command to display the regression output and identify:

-   the estimated slope coefficient

-   the value of $R^2$

-   the value of the **F-statistic**

-   the **degrees of freedom** of the test

```{r}
#| echo: true

summary(model_hw)
```

This output provides important information about the model, including the estimated coefficients, the value of $R^2$, and the F-statistic used for hypothesis testing.

Use the model summary to answer the following questions.

1.  What is the value of $F_{calc}$?

2.  What are the degrees of freedom for the test?

3.  Use the command below to find the critical value $F_{crit}$ at the 5% significance level.

```{r}
#| echo: true

qf(0.95, 1, 83)
```

4.  Compare $F_{calc}$ with $F_{crit}$.
5.  State your conclusion in words.

[**Question 3**]{style="color:red;"}

The F-test indicated that the relationship is statistically significant.

What does this mean in practical terms?

\vspace{3cm}

[**Question 4**]{style="color:red;"}

Why should we be cautious when using regression models to make predictions outside the range of the observed data?

\vspace{3cm}

So far we have focused on the relationship between homework time and homework grades.

In the next part of the exercise you will explore other relationships in the dataset and investigate which variables may help explain student performance.

# Part 2: Exploring Other Relationships

The dataset contains several other variables describing students and their academic performance.

Some variables that may be relevant include:

-   GPA

-   Grade_course

-   Grade_HW

-   HW_minutes

-   Midnight_deadline

Your task is to explore possible relationships between these variables.

Before analysing the relationship formally, it is useful to visualise the data using a scatterplot.

We can then fit a simple linear regression model that describes the relationship between homework time and homework grade.

**Step 1**: Create Scatterplots

Create scatterplots for several pairs of variables.

```{r}
plot(Grade_course ~ GPA, data = hw)

plot(Grade_HW ~ GPA, data = hw)

plot(Grade_course ~ HW_minutes, data = hw)
```

Add the line of best fit to the scatter plots using the `abline()` function as illustrated below.

```{r}
plot(Grade_course ~ GPA,
     data = hw,
     pch = 19,
     col = "steelblue",
     xlab = "GPA",
     ylab = "Course grade")

abline(lm(Grade_course ~ GPA, data = hw),
       col = "red",
       lwd = 2)
```

For each plot consider:

-   Does a relationship appear to exist?

-   Is the relationship positive or negative?

-   Does the relationship appear strong or weak?

**Step 2**: Fit Regression Models

Choose two or three relationships that seem interesting and fit simple regression models.

```{r}
lm(Grade_course ~ GPA, data = hw)

lm(Grade_HW ~ GPA, data = hw)

lm(Grade_course ~ HW_minutes, data = hw)
```

For each model examine:

-   the slope

-   the ($R^2$) value

-   whether the relationship appears statistically significant

[**Question 5**]{style="color:red;"}

Which pair of variables appears to have the strongest relationship?

Explain your reasoning.

\vspace{4cm}

# Part 3: Thinking Ahead

Next week we will build multiple regression models, where several explanatory variables are used together to explain variation in an outcome.

Think about the following question:

If you wanted to explain variation in student grades, which variables from this dataset might be useful predictors?

Write down two or three variables that you would include in a multiple regression model.

\vspace{4cm}

**Optional**: Investigating Correlations

As a quick summary of relationships in the dataset, we can examine the correlation matrix.

```{r}
cor(hw[,c("GPA","Grade_course","Grade_HW","HW_minutes")])
```

Which variables in the dataset might help explain variation in course grades?

\vspace{2cm}

Which variables appear to be most strongly correlated?

\vspace{2cm}

## Exploring Correlations

In the previous exercises you explored several relationships between variables using scatterplots and simple regression models. Another useful way to summarise relationships in a dataset is to examine a **correlation matrix**.

The correlation coefficient measures the **strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables**.\
Its values range from −1 to 1:

\begin{table}[h]
\centering
\begin{tabular}{ll}
\toprule
\textbf{Value} & \textbf{Interpretation} \\
\midrule
$-1$ & strong negative relationship \\
$0$  & no linear relationship \\
$1$  & strong positive relationship \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

A correlation matrix summarises the relationships between several variables simultaneously.

### Computing a Correlation Matrix in R

We first select the numerical variables that may be relevant for academic performance.

```{r}
vars <- hw[, c("HW_minutes",
               "Grade_HW",
               "Grade_course",
               "GPA")]

cor(vars, use = "complete.obs")
```

This command computes the **correlation matrix** between the selected variables. Each value in the table shows the correlation between a pair of variables.

# Your Turn 👉

Examine the correlation matrix and answer the following questions:

1.  Which variables appear to be most strongly correlated?

2.  Which variables appear to have little or no relationship?

3.  Do the correlations support what we observed earlier between homework time and homework grade?

### Looking Ahead

Correlation analysis helps us identify variables that may be useful predictors in a regression model.

Next week we will extend our analysis by fitting a multiple regression model that includes several explanatory variables at the same time.
